statement.markdown: Write a function to find the length of the longest increasing subsequence in a given array of integers. For example, if the input array is [10, 9, 2, 5, 3, 7, 101, 18], the longest increasing subsequence has a length of 4 (the subsequence is [2, 3, 7, 101]). The function should handle arrays of varying lengths and should not use any external libraries or data structures other than arrays and integers.
statement.inputFormat: An array of integers as a comma-separated string or space-separated list, e.g. "10, 9, 2, 5, 3, 7, 101, 18"
statement.outputFormat: An integer representing the length of the longest increasing subsequence
statement.notes: You may assume that the input array is not empty. The function should return 1 if the input array contains a single element.

hints.text: First hint text: Initialize a 2D array to store the lengths of the longest increasing subsequences ending at each position.

editorial.title: Efficient Longest Increasing Subsequence Finder
editorial.intuition: The key idea is to use dynamic programming to build up a table of the lengths of the longest increasing subsequences ending at each position.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve generating all possible subsequences and checking if they are increasing.
editorial.betterSolution: Dynamic programming can be used to build up a table of the lengths of the longest increasing subsequences ending at each position.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves initializing a 2D array and filling it up using a bottom-up dynamic programming approach.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the solution can be proven by showing that the table is filled up correctly and that the longest increasing subsequence is found correctly.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the solution can be performed to show how the table is filled up and how the longest increasing subsequence is found.
commonMistakes: Failing to initialize the table correctly
Failing to fill up the table correctly
